summary:
The Scythian Culture (c. 500–200 BC) was the dominant nomadic civilization of the Eurasian steppe from the Black Sea to the Altai Mountains, renowned for horse mastery, distinctive animal-style art, fierce warrior traditions, and the elaborate burial mounds (kurgans) that have yielded spectacular golden artifacts — and whose interactions with the Greek cities of the Black Sea coast created one of antiquity's most fascinating cultural fusions.

The Scythians, Iranian-speaking pastoralists, ranged across the vast grasslands north of the Black Sea (modern Ukraine, Russia, Kazakhstan). Their military effectiveness rested on mounted archery and tactical mobility — they had repulsed a Persian invasion by Darius the Great through a strategy of constant withdrawal and harassment, never allowing the Persians to bring them to a decisive battle. Their goldwork, produced by Greek craftsmen to Scythian specifications, depicts animals in dynamic, interlocking poses — the 'animal style' that also appears from the Black Sea to China. Herodotus's Histories devotes extensive attention to Scythian customs, including their use of hemp in funeral sweat-baths, warrior women (possibly the origin of Amazon myths), and elaborate royal burials with sacrificed horses and retainers. The kurgan burial tradition spread across Eurasia, connecting Scythian culture to related steppe peoples from Hungary to Mongolia.
